children.name: Pterocles
children.description: Sandgrouse — medium-sized, ground-dwelling birds of arid and semi-arid regions. Despite their pigeon-like appearance, they are in their own order. Their most remarkable adaptation is the ability of males to absorb water in their belly feathers and carry it to their chicks at nests that can be kilometres from the nearest water source. Their cryptic plumage blends perfectly with desert sand and gravel.

children.name: Pterocles alchata
children.description: The most widespread European sandgrouse. Greenish above, white belly, and a distinctive elongated central tail feather. Found in arid steppe and semi-desert in Spain, Portugal, and southern France. Flocks fly to water at dawn and dusk, often many kilometres from feeding areas.

children.name: Pterocles orientalis
children.description: A large sandgrouse with a distinctive black belly and chestnut throat. Found in Iberia, North Africa, the Middle East, and Central Asia. Breeds in arid open country with sparse vegetation. The male has a pale grey crown and neck, the female is more heavily barred.

children.name: Pterocles exustus
children.description: A small sandgrouse found from the Sahel through East Africa to the Indian subcontinent. Chestnut belly, yellow throat patch, and a long pointed tail. Inhabits dry savanna and thorn scrub. Flocks can number several hundred birds.

children.name: Pterocles gutturalis
children.description: Found in eastern and southern Africa. A large sandgrouse with a striking yellow throat patch and heavily spotted upperparts. Inhabits dry savanna and grassland. Males carry water to chicks in their specially adapted belly feathers.

children.name: Pterocles senegallus
children.description: A pale, sandy-coloured sandgrouse of the Sahara and Middle East. Finely spotted above, with a black belly and a distinctive orange face patch. Highly adapted to desert life, able to survive in the driest regions of North Africa.

children.name: Pterocles quadatus
children.description: Found across sub-Saharan Africa from Mauritania to Ethiopia. Named for the four dark bands across the chest. Sandy-brown plumage provides excellent camouflage. Gregarious, forming large flocks at waterholes during the dry season.

children.name: Pterocles namaquensis
children.description: A striking sandgrouse of southern Africa. The male has a blue-grey head and breast with a brick-red throat patch. Found in the Namib Desert and arid regions of Botswana and South Africa. One of the most abundant sandgrouse in southern Africa.

children.name: Syrrhaptes
children.description: Pallas's sandgrouse — two species adapted to the high steppes and plateaus of Central Asia. They have feathered legs and feet adapted for walking on snow, and their chicks are born with a dense down that protects them from the cold. S. paradoxus is famous for irregular mass irruptions into western Europe.

children.name: Syrrhaptes paradoxus
children.description: A sandgrouse of Central Asian steppes, famous for periodic mass irruptions into Europe, including Sweden. Sandy-buff with a grey head, black belly, and long, pointed tail feathers. Both legs and feet are feathered. The irruption of 1888 reached the British Isles, with flocks recorded across Sweden and Norway.

children.name: Syrrhaptes tibetanus
children.description: Found on the high Tibetan Plateau at elevations of 4,000 to 6,000 metres. The largest sandgrouse species. Buff above, white below, with a grey head and extensive barring. Adapted to survive in one of the most extreme environments on Earth.
